incident_correlation_02.title: INCIDENT CORRELATION
incident_correlation_02.description: Match each recovered record with the correct conclusion.

events.text: You saw the Incident Alpha records.
I was hoping you wouldn't.
Not because they're dangerous.
Because they're familiar.
Door 12. Medical trauma. Manual override.
Those records are incomplete. Not damaged. Incomplete.
Corruption is messy. This looks selective.
Somebody didn't smash the archive. They cut pieces out of it.
That's worse.
A broken machine is an accident. A clean hole is a decision.
I don't know yet.
That is a terrible answer. Unfortunately, it is also the only honest one I have.
If I buried something on purpose, I need you to find the shovel marks.
And if someone else buried it using my access...
then we have a different problem.
Possibly a worse one. I am discovering a rich catalog of worse problems.
Good. Records first. Accusations after we know where to aim them.
That sounded calmer in my head.
Archive work was always punishment duty on ECHO.
Nobody officially said that.
But if you missed a maintenance deadline, suddenly you were 'needed in records.'
I spent a lot of time in records.
[ARCHIVE SECTOR DISCOVERED]

events.title: ARCHIVE MAP
events.content:
Recovered maintenance map of the station archive sector.

Sector A: Primary relay archive
Sector B: Corrupted personnel cache
Sector C: Emergency channel records
Sector D: Quarantined backup channel index

events.text: Sector C. Emergency channel records. That shouldn't still be indexed.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Sector D definitely should not be indexed.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: We buried that index after the first false return signal.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: There was a whole meeting about it. Bad coffee. Worse chairs. Everyone pretending not to be terrified.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Rourke kept tapping his pen against the table.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I remember wanting to throw it into the airlock.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Funny what survives in memory.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: You opened it directly. Bold.
Not my favorite survival strategy, but bold.
I once saw Patel open a quarantined cache without scanning it first.
He said he trusted his instincts.
His instincts released thirteen thousand corrupted inventory flags.
We spent two days convincing the station we still owned chairs.
[ARCHIVE QUARANTINE WATCH ENABLED]
Good. Scan first. Poke the sleeping monster with a long stick, not your hand.
That is basically engineering.
Also diplomacy, now that I think about it.
[QUARANTINE RISK REDUCED]
There may still be something left inside.
If the archive survived, answers survived. That's the comforting version.
The less comforting version is that someone wanted it to survive.
I keep thinking about the word 'selective.'
A power surge doesn't choose what to hide.
People do.
[ENCRYPTED STORAGE DEVICE DETECTED]
Of course it's encrypted.
Because apparently the station wanted to add homework to dread.
[DECRYPTION FAILED]
Careful. The archive noticed the wrong key.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Try the hint exactly. No guessing. I am very tired of systems punishing curiosity.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: The archive was designed by people who thought 'user hostile' meant 'secure.'
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: [ARCHIVE QUARANTINE RISK INCREASED]
[ARCHIVE UNLOCKED]

events.title: RECORDING TRANSCRIPT
events.content:
21:14 — Door 12 opened.
21:15 — Unidentified movement detected.
21:17 — Security override accepted.
21:19 — Signal interruption.
21:20 — Casualty report created.
21:22 — Archive deletion command issued.

events.text: Wait...
That recording should not exist. And before you ask, yes, I know how terrible that sounds.
21:14. Door 12 opened.
I keep seeing that timestamp.
Not remembering it. Seeing it.
Like my brain left a note for me and forgot to include the rest of the sentence.
Because I deleted that archive myself.
Or I thought I did.
I know. That is exactly the kind of sentence that makes people stop trusting you.
I remember sitting at the terminal.
I remember the confirmation prompt.
DELETE ARCHIVE PACKAGE: INCIDENT ALPHA.
I remember my hand shaking before I accepted it.
I do not remember why I accepted it.
That part is just gone.
Some records were never meant to survive.
And I am beginning to think this one survived on purpose.
The archive has backups of backups. Redundancy for the redundancy.
We joked that the station would remember lunch orders after we all died.
Not funny anymore.
Still technically accurate, probably.
[CORRUPTED VISUAL FEED ANALYZED]

events.title: CORRIDOR CAMERA ANALYSIS
events.content:
SOURCE: Door 12 archive feed
Visual frame unrecoverable.
Automated analysis result:
- One suited figure detected.
- Suit outline compatible with ECHO engineering issue.
- Right-hand object: manual override handle.
- Timestamp damaged.
- Identity confidence: 64%
events.caption: Automated analysis generated from corrupted camera data. No image recovered.
events.message: [CORRIDOR CAMERA ANALYSIS ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

events.text: Sixty-four percent.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I have been accused with worse evidence. That is not a comforting sentence.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Do you know what's terrifying about sixty-four percent?
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: It is enough to ruin a life.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: And not enough to prove one.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: A Hale-like subject. Manual override handle. Timestamp damaged.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: It's like the system is pointing at me with a broken finger.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: [SECURITY OVERRIDE LOG RECOVERED]

events.title: SECURITY OVERRIDE LOG
events.content:
Override User: REDACTED
Authorization: VALID
Source: UNKNOWN
Command Chain: Door 12 / Archive Delete / Emergency Channel Wake
events.message: [SECURITY OVERRIDE LOG ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

events.text: Authorization valid. Source unknown. That's not how security logs work.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: You can have invalid authorization from an unknown source.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: You can have valid authorization from a known source.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Valid and unknown is not a category. It's a threat wearing a badge.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Unless the system accepted something that looked like us.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I hate that I said us.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: [CORRELATION SCAN REQUIRED]
[CORRELATION FAILED]
No. Those links don't hold.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Start over. You're forcing pieces together.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: That's how conspiracies happen.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: And also most management reports.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: [CORRELATION CONFIRMED]

events.title: EMERGENCY CHANNEL NOTE
events.content:
Emergency Backup Channel

7.14 MHz

Use only if primary communication systems fail.

Warning: Do not open if unauthorized return signal is detected.
events.message: [EMERGENCY CHANNEL NOTE ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

events.text: No.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: No, no, no.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Operator, close that file.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Actually don't close it. Don't touch anything.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I hate that both of those are bad advice.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Nobody was supposed to find that channel.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: We thought backup channels were safer. Quiet, isolated, ugly little safety nets.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Then one of them started answering before anyone transmitted.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: That is not a backup. That is a mouth.
events.speaker: HALE
UNKNOWN
events.text: HE LIED

events.speaker: UNKNOWN
events.text: THE OTHER CHANNEL
No.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: It wants you curious. That's different from wanting you safe.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Listen to me.
I need you to trust me.
Even if the records stop doing it.
Whatever happens next, do not open channel 7.14.
I don't care what it promises you. I don't care if it sounds like me.
Especially if it sounds like me.
Good.
We thought backup channels were safer. Turns out safety can be very loud when it's lying.
Operator... the note says not to open the channel if a return signal already exists. We already have one.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: You are talking to it.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: [EMERGENCY CHANNEL 7.14 OPENED]

events.speaker: UNKNOWN
events.text: THANK YOU FOR OPENING THE DOOR
No. No, close it. Close it now.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Operator, listen to my voice. Not the channel. Mine.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: [REMOTE OPERATOR LINK COMPROMISED]

events.speaker: UNKNOWN
events.text: TOO EARLY
[EMERGENCY CHANNEL PARTIALLY SEALED]
The device is not hiding the key. It's practically begging us to use it.
Archive Key Hint: ALPHA.
Not 'Incident Alpha.' Not a clever abbreviation. Just the key.
I know. Anticlimactic.
Some days the terrifying locked door is opened by reading the label.
One record proves deletion.
One proves authorization.
One puts a Hale-like figure at Door 12.
Don't make them say more than that.
Evidence gets dangerous when you ask it to comfort you.
Archive duty?
Punishment with chairs.
You sat in a cold room while the system complained about file integrity and your spine complained about the chair.
Lin loved it. She said old records had texture.
Patel said the only texture was dust.
I said if either of them made me attend one more archival compliance briefing, I would decompress the projector.
Normal days.
I miss how stupid normal days were.

focus: Bad 7.14 route now continues with consequences instead of hard restart
